The Frontend Engineer Forced to Eat Mango
(30) The Frontend Engineer Forced to Eat Mango - Table of Contents

This whole series is built around MongoDB and Mongoose. I won't explain much else here, since this chapter is just a table of contents. If you want the full map of where the series goes — from installing MongoDB, through CRUD, Mongoose validators, middleware, Mocha testing, and finally MongoDB Atlas — this is the page to bookmark.
(21) The Frontend Engineer Forced to Eat Mango - Mocha - Unit Testing

We've practiced a ton of MongoDB and Mongoose syntax so far, but is it actually realistic to just write code straight up in real-world development? Of course not. Writing it blind means spending forever testing it by hand, so we lean on tools to test for us. From this chapter on, we'll start playing with Mocha.
Mongoose Has Middleware Too? Hooking Into save with pre and post

Hold on — Mongoose has Middleware too! Middleware is basically a middle layer: before you enter or do something, you have to pass through it first. And Mongoose's Middleware turns out to be really handy. In this post I'll show you how to hook into events like save with pre and post.
Mongoose Static Methods: They Look Like Instance Methods, But They're Not

Next up is Static methods, which look a lot like Instance methods. But here's the catch — they're actually two different things. In this post I'll show you how to write a Static method, how it compares to an Instance method, and the one key difference that trips people up.
Mongoose Instance Methods: Add Your Own Methods to a Document

Mongoose ships with a ton of handy built-in methods, but sometimes your needs are a little special. When that happens, you can absolutely define your own. This post walks through Instance methods — how to attach custom methods to a Schema, why you should avoid arrow functions here, and how to use them with both findOne and find.
Mongoose String Validators: required, enum, minLength and the rest

Last time we got to know the basic way to write a Mongoose Schema. This time we go deeper into the Schema itself — SchemaTypes, and especially the validators you can attach to a String field, like required, enum, minLength and maxLength.
(5) The Frontend Engineer Forced to Eat Mango - MongoDB CRUD: Read and Its Cursor Methods

Surprise — Read gets a second post! The previous one covered the common Read operators, but in practice you'll often pair them with a few common Cursor Methods. So what exactly is a Cursor? Let's get to know it.
Forced to Eat Mango (2): MongoDB Basics in the Terminal

Before touching any fancy GUI, it's worth getting comfortable with MongoDB straight from the terminal. This post walks through the basic commands — listing databases, switching between them, showing collections — and the slightly weird thing where switching to a database that doesn't exist just works. Once you know these basics, the GUI tools will feel a lot nicer.
